explanation: Halkbank: FSIA addresses civil immunity; criminal proceedings are governed by common-law foreign-sovereign-immunity principles.

stem: Under the Anti-Injunction Act (28 U.S.C. § 2283), federal courts may NOT enjoin state-court proceedings except:
opt_a: When the state-court judgment would be res judicata in federal court
opt_b: When expressly authorized by Congress, in aid of jurisdiction, or to protect/effectuate federal judgments
opt_c: Whenever federal law preempts state law
opt_d: When the state proceeding violates the Equal Protection Clause
explanation: § 2283: three express exceptions. Mitchum v. Foster held § 1983 is 'expressly authorized' by Congress for this purpose.

stem: The Rooker-Feldman doctrine bars federal-court review of:
opt_a: All cases in which state courts have ruled on federal-law issues
opt_b: Cases parallel to ongoing state proceedings
opt_c: State-court judgments in cases where the federal-court plaintiff is the losing party in state court and seeks to appeal that judgment
opt_d: Any federal claim that was raised in state court
explanation: Rooker-Feldman: federal district courts lack jurisdiction over state-court 'losers' seeking to overturn the state-court judgment. Exxon Mobil v. Saudi Basic narrowed the doctrine.

stem: Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Industries (2005) narrowed Rooker-Feldman by holding it applies only to:
opt_a: Any case touching on a state-court determination
opt_b: Cases brought by state-court losers complaining of injuries caused by state-court judgments and inviting district-court review
opt_c: Cases parallel to ongoing state proceedings
opt_d: Federal claims that should have been raised in state court
explanation: Exxon Mobil v. Saudi Basic: narrowed Rooker-Feldman significantly. Most parallel state-federal litigation is not barred.

stem: Wilton v. Seven Falls Co. (1995) clarified federal-court discretion to entertain declaratory-judgment actions when:
opt_a: The federal court may entertain DJ actions only with consent of all parties
opt_b: A parallel state-court action is pending; federal courts have substantial discretion to decline DJ jurisdiction in favor of state proceedings
opt_c: Federal courts have parallel state-law proceedings only in diversity cases
opt_d: Federal courts must always defer to state DJ proceedings
explanation: Wilton: federal courts have substantial discretion in DJ actions when parallel state proceedings exist. Brillhart abstention factors guide the analysis.

stem: The Tax Anti-Injunction Act (26 U.S.C. § 7421) generally bars federal-court suits to enjoin tax assessment or collection. The exception in South Carolina v. Regan permits:
opt_a: Suits by state attorneys general
opt_b: Suits seeking declaratory rather than injunctive relief
opt_c: Class actions challenging tax assessments
opt_d: Federal-court suits when no other adequate forum exists for the claim
explanation: Tax Anti-Injunction Act: bars suits to enjoin tax. South Carolina v. Regan recognized a narrow exception when no other adequate forum exists.

stem: Skelly Oil v. Phillips Petroleum (1950) extended Mottley's well-pleaded complaint rule to:
opt_a: All federal-question cases
opt_b: Declaratory-judgment plaintiffs, who are tested as if they were the natural-coercive-action plaintiff
opt_c: Removal jurisdiction only
opt_d: Cases involving federal regulations
explanation: Skelly Oil: declaratory-judgment plaintiffs cannot create FQJ via DJ procedure where the underlying coercive suit would not be a federal-question case.

stem: Forum non conveniens (FNC) permits federal courts to dismiss a case in favor of a more convenient foreign forum. The doctrine:
opt_a: Is inapplicable in federal-question cases
opt_b: Requires consent of all parties
opt_c: Is preempted by § 1404(a)
opt_d: Differs from § 1404(a) transfer in being applicable to non-U.S. forums and resulting in dismissal
explanation: FNC: addresses non-U.S. forums; § 1404(a) addresses domestic transfers. FNC dismissals are with prejudice (subject to refiling abroad).

stem: For diversity-jurisdiction purposes, an inactive subsidiary corporation:
opt_a: Has no citizenship for diversity purposes
opt_b: Is treated as a partnership of its directors
opt_c: Is automatically a citizen wherever its parent is
opt_d: Is generally a citizen of its state of incorporation; its 'nerve center' may be the parent's headquarters or another location
explanation: Hertz applied to inactive corporations: generally state of incorporation + nerve center, even if 'nerve' is somewhat thin.

stem: § 1369 confers federal jurisdiction over multiparty mass-disaster cases involving:
opt_a: Federally-regulated transportation incidents
opt_b: Maritime mass-disaster cases
opt_c: 75+ deaths from a single accident, with minimal diversity among the parties
opt_d: Class actions involving more than 100 plaintiffs
explanation: § 1369: '75+ deaths' + minimal-diversity multiparty multiforum jurisdiction.

stem: Removal generally requires unanimous consent of all properly-joined defendants. Exceptions include:
opt_a: All federal-question cases
opt_b: Removals filed within 7 days of service
opt_c: Cases involving federal-tort claims
opt_d: Federal-officer removal (§ 1442) and CAFA class actions
explanation: Removal unanimity rule applies to most cases. § 1442 (federal-officer) and CAFA (class actions) are notable exceptions.

stem: Younger v. Harris held that federal courts must abstain from enjoining ongoing state criminal proceedings. The doctrine rests on:
opt_a: The political-question doctrine
opt_b: Limitations on federal-court remedial power
opt_c: Equity, comity, and federalism — federal courts should not interfere with state law-enforcement
opt_d: Article III's case-or-controversy requirement
explanation: Younger abstention: a 'Our Federalism' doctrine rooted in equity and respect for state authority over criminal proceedings.

stem: Pullman abstention (Railroad Comm'n v. Pullman) requires federal courts to abstain when:
opt_a: A federal constitutional question depends on an unsettled state-law question that state courts could resolve
opt_b: The federal claim implicates significant state interests
opt_c: The state has expressly invoked abstention
opt_d: State courts have parallel proceedings on similar facts
explanation: Pullman: federal courts stay constitutional adjudication if state-court resolution of a state-law question could obviate the federal issue.

stem: Colorado River abstention permits federal courts to defer to parallel state proceedings only:
opt_a: Whenever a parallel state proceeding exists
opt_b: In 'exceptional circumstances' weighing comprehensive disposition, convenience, avoidance of piecemeal litigation, etc.
opt_c: In water-rights cases only
opt_d: With consent of all parties
explanation: Colorado River: a narrow abstention doctrine for parallel federal-state litigation. Limited to 'exceptional circumstances.'

stem: Burford abstention applies when federal-court resolution would:
opt_a: Conflict with parallel state proceedings
opt_b: Force state-court litigants to relitigate federal claims
opt_c: Disrupt complex state regulatory schemes involving substantial state interests
opt_d: Implicate state-court interpretations of federal law
explanation: Burford abstention: federal courts decline jurisdiction to avoid disrupting complex state regulatory frameworks.

stem: The Rooker-Feldman doctrine bars federal-district-court jurisdiction over:
opt_a: Federal-question cases also litigated in state court
opt_b: Cases brought after state-court judgments became final
opt_c: Any case touching on a state-court ruling
opt_d: Cases by state-court losers seeking to overturn state-court judgments
explanation: Rooker-Feldman: federal district courts cannot review state-court judgments. Exxon Mobil narrowed to true 'losers' seeking review.

stem: Atascadero State Hospital v. Scanlon (1985) established that congressional abrogation of state sovereign immunity must be:
opt_a: In a separate statute apart from the substantive provision
opt_b: Within Congress's § 5 enforcement powers
opt_c: Approved by a state-immunity certification
opt_d: Unmistakably clear in the statutory language
explanation: Atascadero: Congress must use 'unmistakably clear' language to abrogate state immunity. Foundational clear-statement rule.

stem: In Felder v. Casey, Wisconsin's pre-suit notice requirement was preempted in § 1983 actions because:
opt_a: It exceeded Congress's § 5 abrogation authority
opt_b: It conflicted with FRCP 8's pleading standards
opt_c: It violated the Equal Protection Clause
opt_d: It was outcome-determinative and burdened federal-law rights
explanation: Felder: state procedural rules that are outcome-determinative and burden § 1983 rights are preempted in state court.

stem: In Howlett v. Rose, the Court held that state-law sovereign immunity:
opt_a: Applies in federal court but not state court
opt_b: Cannot defeat § 1983 personal-capacity damages claims; federal-law immunities (Harlow QI) govern
opt_c: Categorically defeats § 1983 claims in state court
opt_d: Applies only to common-law tort actions
explanation: Howlett v. Rose: state courts cannot use state-law immunities to defeat § 1983; federal law governs § 1983 immunity questions.

stem: Indiana ex rel. Anderson v. Brand permitted Supreme Court review of state-law antecedent issues that:
opt_a: Determine federal rights, with deference to but not absolute fidelity to the state-court interpretation
opt_b: Have been overruled by the state's own intermediate court
opt_c: Are independent and adequate state grounds
opt_d: Apply only to property cases
explanation: Brand: state-law antecedents that determine federal rights are reviewable with deference but not absolute fidelity. Murdock applies to pure state-law disputes.

stem: Michigan v. Long created a bright-line presumption of federal-law reliance applicable when:
opt_a: The state court explicitly invokes federal grounds
opt_b: The state-court opinion fairly appears to rest on federal law and is not clearly independent
opt_c: The state-court opinion mentions federal law in any capacity
opt_d: The state court certifies a federal question for review
explanation: Long: bright-line presumption favoring federal review unless the state court explicitly invokes independent state grounds.

stem: Erie R.R. v. Tompkins held that, in diversity cases, federal courts must apply:
opt_a: Federal general common law of commercial transactions
opt_b: Forum-state procedural rules but federal substantive common law
opt_c: State substantive law, including state common law
opt_d: Federal common law unless Congress directs otherwise
explanation: Brandeis: there is no federal general common law in diversity. State substantive law controls (subject to FCL enclaves).

stem: Hanna v. Plumer's two-track analysis distinguishes:
opt_a: Cases in federal vs state court
opt_b: Cases involving citizens of different states
opt_c: FRCP rule cases (REA-authorized — apply if valid) from unguided Erie choices (apply outcome-determinative test)
opt_d: Cases involving substantive vs procedural law
explanation: Hanna's two-track: REA-authorized FRCP rules apply over conflicting state law; unguided Erie choices apply the modified outcome-determinative test.

stem: Boyle v. United Technologies's three-part government-contractor defense applies when:
opt_a: The FTCA expressly authorizes the defense
opt_b: The contract includes an express indemnity clause
opt_c: The U.S. provided reasonably precise specifications, the contractor conformed, and warned of dangers known to it
opt_d: The contractor is a federal employee or agency
explanation: Boyle: three-part test for federal-common-law preemption of state product-liability claims against defense contractors.

stem: Banco Nacional v. Sabbatino held the act-of-state doctrine is federal common law applicable to:
opt_a: Recognition of foreign judgments in U.S. courts
opt_b: Any commercial transaction involving foreign sovereigns
opt_c: Validity of foreign sovereign acts taken within the foreign sovereign's territory
opt_d: Treaty-implementation disputes
explanation: Sabbatino: act-of-state generally bars inquiry into the validity of foreign sovereign acts within their own territory; federal common law from foreign-relations interests.

stem: Walker v. Armco Steel held that, in a diversity action, the federal court should apply:
opt_a: FRCP 3 (filing) as the commencement event
opt_b: The longer of state and federal limitations periods
opt_c: State law governing when a state-law claim is 'commenced' for SOL purposes (service vs filing)
opt_d: Federal common law of SOL accrual
explanation: Walker v. Armco: state law governs SOL commencement (service vs filing) in diversity actions — substantive/outcome-determinative for Erie.

stem: Stewart Org. v. Ricoh held that 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) controls over conflicting state forum-selection-clause law because:
opt_a: § 1404 is part of the FRCP
opt_b: Venue is exclusively federal-law
opt_c: § 1404(a) is a valid federal procedural statute that supplants state law on venue transfer
opt_d: Forum-selection clauses are procedural under Erie
explanation: Stewart Org. v. Ricoh: federal venue-transfer law controls over conflicting state law in diversity.

stem: Gasperini v. Center for Humanities held that a federal court in diversity must apply state law on:
opt_a: Lincoln Mills authorizes federal common-law development under § 301
opt_b: Federal-law standards for jury review under FRCP 59
opt_c: Whichever standard is more deferential
opt_d: The substance of jury-verdict review for excessiveness, with federal-law review standards
explanation: Gasperini: state law on jury-verdict review is substantive for Erie; federal courts apply state-law-influenced standards while applying federal review.

stem: Shady Grove Orthopedic v. Allstate held that FRCP 23 controls over conflicting state law that limits class-action availability because:
opt_a: State limits violate Equal Protection
opt_b: Federal courts have discretion to ignore state class-action limits
opt_c: Class actions are uniquely federal
opt_d: FRCP 23 is a valid REA rule that permits class actions in federal court regardless of state limits
explanation: Shady Grove: FRCP 23 controls over conflicting state law; the Court split on the precise rationale.

stem: Klaxon v. Stentor held that, in diversity actions, federal courts must apply:
opt_a: The choice-of-law rules of the state in which the federal court sits
opt_b: The choice-of-law rule of the state with the most significant relationship
opt_c: Federal common-law choice-of-law principles
opt_d: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws
explanation: Klaxon: in diversity, federal courts apply forum-state choice-of-law rules. Erie governs choice-of-law as well as substantive law.

stem: Day & Zimmermann v. Challoner reaffirmed Klaxon despite arguments for federal common-law choice-of-law uniformity. The Court reasoned:
opt_a: Choice-of-law is exclusively state-law
opt_b: Erie's anti-forum-shopping principles require federal courts to apply state choice-of-law rules
opt_c: Federal courts lack authority to develop common-law choice-of-law rules
opt_d: Federal common law would be administratively complex
explanation: Day & Zimmermann: reaffirmed Klaxon. Federal courts in diversity apply forum-state choice-of-law rules.

stem: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents recognized an implied damages remedy for:
opt_a: Fifth Amendment due-process violations
opt_b: First Amendment retaliation
opt_c: Fourth Amendment violations by federal officers in the home
opt_d: Eighth Amendment medical claims
explanation: Bivens's narrow original holding: 4A damages remedy for federal-officer warrantless search.

stem: After Egbert v. Boule, a federal court considering whether to extend Bivens asks:
opt_a: Whether the constitutional right is clearly established
opt_b: Whether special factors counsel hesitation
opt_c: Whether any rational reason exists for political-branch handling, in which case extension fails
opt_d: Whether alternative remedies are adequate substitutes for damages
explanation: Egbert collapsed the test: any rational reason for Congressional preference defeats Bivens extension.

stem: Davis v. Passman extended Bivens because the plaintiff:
opt_a: Was a federal-prison inmate denied medical care
opt_b: Was a NASA engineer demoted for whistleblowing
opt_c: Was a non-citizen detained at the border
opt_d: Was a Congressional staffer fired on grounds of sex, and Title VII expressly excluded Congressional staff
explanation: Davis: Title VII's exclusion of Congressional staff left plaintiff with no federal remedy.

stem: Carlson v. Green held Bivens applied to an Eighth Amendment medical-care claim. The Court reasoned FTCA was insufficient because:
opt_a: Bivens damages directly against officers serve different functions than FTCA recovery (state-law-based)
opt_b: Plaintiff sought injunctive relief that FTCA does not authorize
opt_c: FTCA does not permit suits arising in federal prisons
opt_d: FTCA's exclusive-remedy provisions had not yet been enacted
explanation: Carlson: Bivens and FTCA serve different purposes. Bivens provides uniform federal damages directly against officers.

stem: Cannon v. University of Chicago held Title IX includes an implied PROA for:
opt_a: Sex discrimination only at federally-funded schools
opt_b: Disparate-impact sex discrimination
opt_c: Intentional sex discrimination, given rights-creating language and Congressional intent
opt_d: Both intentional and disparate-impact
explanation: Cannon: Title IX implies PROA for intentional sex discrimination. Sandoval cabined to intentional only.

stem: Sandoval shifted the implied-PROA inquiry from Cort v. Ash's purposivism to:
opt_a: Legislative history primarily
opt_b: Constitutional-avoidance principles
opt_c: Text and structure of the statute, considering whether Congress used rights-creating language
opt_d: Agency-deference analysis
explanation: Sandoval: shifted to textual/structural analysis. Cort's purposivist factors largely displaced.

stem: In Health & Hospital Corp. v. Talevski (2023), the Court held that Spending-Clause statutes can support § 1983 enforcement when:
opt_a: The statute creates categorical entitlement to damages
opt_b: The text uses unambiguous rights-creating language and there is no comprehensive remedial scheme that displaces § 1983
opt_c: The state has accepted federal funds
opt_d: The agency expressly authorizes private suits
explanation: Talevski: rights-creating language plus absence of an exclusive remedial scheme.

stem: A U.S. Border Patrol agent shoots and kills a Mexican national across the border. The family sues under Bivens. After Hernandez v. Mesa:
opt_a: No Bivens action lies because cross-border shootings present a new context with foreign-relations special factors
opt_b: The family's exclusive remedy is diplomatic resolution
opt_c: Bivens applies because the U.S. Constitution reaches federal officers' conduct extraterritorially
opt_d: Bivens applies for the Fourth Amendment but not the Fifth
explanation: Hernandez v. Mesa: cross-border + foreign-relations special factors defeat Bivens extension.

stem: Bush v. Lucas declined Bivens because:
opt_a: The federal civil-service remedial scheme provided an adequate alternative
opt_b: Congress had specifically barred Bivens
opt_c: Government employment is a privilege, not a right
opt_d: The First Amendment is not self-executing
explanation: Bush v. Lucas: adequate alternative remedies (civil-service review) defeat Bivens extension.

stem: Wilkie v. Robbins declined to extend Bivens to a rancher's harassment claims. A central reason was:
opt_a: Failure to exhaust administrative remedies
opt_b: Property-rights disputes are exclusively state law
opt_c: Federal officers lack capacity to harass
opt_d: The unworkability of an unconstitutional-retaliation Bivens action without a clear standard
explanation: Wilkie v. Robbins: 'special factors' include administrability — a freestanding retaliation claim required courts to police every federal-officer interaction.

stem: Atascadero State Hospital v. Scanlon established that congressional abrogation of state sovereign immunity must:
opt_a: Be approved by a state-immunity certification
opt_b: Be expressed in unmistakably clear language in the statute
opt_c: Be in a separate statute apart from the substantive provision
opt_d: Have Senate supermajority approval
explanation: Atascadero: clear-statement rule. Congress must use 'unmistakably clear' language to abrogate state immunity.

stem: Edelman v. Jordan distinguishes Ex parte Young from suits seeking:
opt_a: Declaratory judgment about state federal-law obligations
opt_b: Injunctive relief against state officers in personal capacity
opt_c: Prospective compliance with federal law
opt_d: Retroactive money damages payable from the state treasury
explanation: Edelman: Young permits prospective relief but bars retroactive damages from state treasury.

stem: Pennhurst v. Halderman held Ex parte Young does NOT permit:
opt_a: Federal-court injunctive enforcement of state law against state officers
opt_b: Class-action habeas claims
opt_c: Damages claims against state officers in personal capacity
opt_d: Federal-question suits against state officers
explanation: Pennhurst: Young is for federal-law enforcement; using it to enforce state law against state officials would intrude on state sovereignty.

stem: After Seminole Tribe v. Florida, Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity:
opt_a: Under any enumerated power
opt_b: Only under § 5 of the 14th Amendment, not under Article I commerce powers
opt_c: With state-immunity certification approval
opt_d: Whenever it makes a clear statement
explanation: Seminole Tribe: Article I powers cannot abrogate SSI; § 5 of the 14th Amendment can.

stem: Nevada Department of Human Resources v. Hibbs upheld FMLA's family-care provision because:
opt_a: FMLA is exclusively administrative
opt_b: Commerce-power abrogation is permissible for employment statutes
opt_c: States waived SSI by accepting federal funds
opt_d: It validly abrogated SSI under § 5 to remedy historical sex discrimination in family leave
explanation: Hibbs: FMLA family-care provision was upheld as valid § 5 abrogation; congruence-and-proportionality satisfied by historical record.

stem: Tennessee v. Lane held Title II of the ADA validly abrogates SSI as applied to:
opt_a: Cases where the state has accepted federal disability-related funds
opt_b: All disability-discrimination cases against states
opt_c: Cases involving fundamental rights, such as access to courts
opt_d: Only public-employment disability cases
explanation: Lane: Title II abrogation is valid as applied to fundamental-rights cases.

stem: Central Virginia Community College v. Katz upheld bankruptcy proceedings against state interests because:
opt_a: Congress validly abrogated SSI under § 5
opt_b: 11A does not address Article I bankruptcy
opt_c: States consented to federal bankruptcy jurisdiction in the plan of the convention
opt_d: Bankruptcy is a public-rights matter outside state immunity
explanation: Katz: ratification of the Bankruptcy Clause implicitly waived state immunity for bankruptcy proceedings — plan of convention.

stem: Torres v. Texas Department of Public Safety extended plan-of-convention reasoning to:
opt_a: Suits enforcing the Spending Clause
opt_b: Suits enforcing federal employment protections under congressional war powers
opt_c: Suits enforcing federal admiralty law against state actors
opt_d: Suits enforcing the Equal Protection Clause directly
explanation: Torres: USERRA suits against states allowed under war-power-based plan-of-convention abrogation.

stem: Monroe v. Pape held that § 1983 reaches:
opt_a: Only state officials acting within authority
opt_b: Federal officials acting jointly with state
opt_c: State officials acting under color of state law, even when violating state law
opt_d: Only state officials in official capacity
explanation: Monroe: 'under color of' includes abuses of authority. Foundational § 1983 scope.

stem: A county prosecutor authorizes a particular police entry; the entry is later held unconstitutional. Under Pembaur v. Cincinnati:
opt_a: The county is shielded by sovereign immunity
opt_b: The county is liable only with a pattern of similar decisions
opt_c: The single decision constitutes county 'policy' if the prosecutor was the final policymaker
opt_d: Only the prosecutor personally is liable
explanation: Pembaur: a single decision by a final policymaker can constitute Monell 'policy.'

stem: Williams v. Taylor distinguished § 2254(d)(1)'s 'contrary to' from 'unreasonable application' as follows:
opt_a: 'Contrary to' is reviewed de novo; 'unreasonable application' is for clear error
opt_b: 'Contrary to' applies to facts; 'unreasonable application' applies to law
opt_c: The two prongs are functionally identical
opt_d: 'Contrary to' applies when the state court reached opposite results on materially indistinguishable facts; 'unreasonable application' applies when the rule was correctly identified but applied unreasonably
explanation: Williams' canonical articulation. 'Contrary to' = wrong rule or opposite outcome on indistinguishable facts. 'Unreasonable application' = right rule, unreasonable application.

stem: Cullen v. Pinholster restricted federal habeas review under § 2254(d)(1) to:
opt_a: The combined state and federal records
opt_b: The record before the state court at the time of its merits adjudication
opt_c: Any record developed in state collateral review
opt_d: The federal-habeas record, including evidence developed in federal court
explanation: Pinholster: § 2254(d)(1) review limited to the state-court record. New-evidence development sharply curtailed.

stem: A defendant fails to object at trial to coerced-confession evidence; the state appellate court denies the claim under a state-procedural-default rule. The federal habeas court should:
opt_a: Defer to the state-court procedural ruling without further inquiry
opt_b: Apply AEDPA's § 2254(d)(1) standard to the procedural default itself
opt_c: Reach the merits because federal courts review federal claims de novo
opt_d: Apply the cause-and-prejudice standard to determine whether to excuse the procedural default
explanation: Wainwright v. Sykes: cause-and-prejudice (or actual-innocence gateway) is the standard for excusing procedural default.

stem: A federal habeas petitioner argues that his collateral-review counsel was ineffective. Under Coleman v. Thompson:
opt_a: Collateral-review attorney error is always 'cause'
opt_b: Collateral-review counsel's negligence triggers a Strickland-style federal habeas analysis
opt_c: Collateral-review attorney error is generally not 'cause' to excuse default, with a narrow Martinez exception
opt_d: Collateral-review attorney error excuses default if the underlying claim is colorable
explanation: Coleman: no constitutional right to collateral-review counsel means error there isn't 'cause' (Martinez carved a narrow exception).

stem: In an Arizona-style state where IATC claims must be raised first on initial-review collateral, the petitioner's collateral-review counsel was ineffective. Under Martinez v. Ryan:
opt_a: Ineffective collateral-review counsel may serve as 'cause' to excuse default of the substantial IATC claim
opt_b: The procedural default is automatically excused because no other forum was available
opt_c: Ineffective collateral-review counsel is never 'cause' under Coleman
opt_d: The petitioner must independently establish actual innocence
explanation: Martinez v. Ryan: where state law channels IATC to initial-review collateral, ineffective counsel there can be 'cause.'

stem: After a state conviction becomes final, the U.S. Supreme Court announces a new constitutional rule of criminal procedure regarding jury selection. Under Teague v. Lane:
opt_a: The new rule generally does not apply on collateral review unless it falls within the substantive-rule or watershed-procedural exceptions
opt_b: The new rule applies on collateral review only to capital cases
opt_c: The new rule applies on collateral review because federal courts apply current constitutional law
opt_d: The new rule applies on collateral review only if the state expressly opts in
explanation: Teague: new procedural rules don't apply retroactively on collateral review except for narrow exceptions.

stem: A state prisoner whose Fourth Amendment exclusionary-rule claim was fully litigated in state court seeks federal habeas relief. Under Stone v. Powell:
opt_a: Federal habeas relief is unavailable because the state provided full and fair litigation opportunity
opt_b: Federal habeas review proceeds de novo because 4A claims are core
opt_c: Federal habeas relief is available only if the petitioner is actually innocent
opt_d: Federal habeas review proceeds under § 2254(d)(1) deference
explanation: Stone v. Powell: 4A exclusionary-rule claims aren't cognizable on habeas where the state gave full and fair litigation opportunity.

stem: A petitioner files federal habeas more than one year after conviction-finality but produces credible new affidavits showing actual innocence. Under McQuiggin v. Perkins:
opt_a: Actual innocence excuses procedural default but not the AEDPA SOL
opt_b: The actual-innocence showing equitably tolls AEDPA's one-year statute of limitations
opt_c: The petition must be dismissed because AEDPA's SOL is jurisdictional
opt_d: The petitioner must produce DNA evidence
explanation: McQuiggin: actual-innocence claim under Schlup serves as an equitable gateway through AEDPA's SOL.

stem: In Boumediene v. Bush, the Court held:
opt_a: The Suspension Clause limits Congress's ability to strip habeas jurisdiction over Guantanamo detainees
opt_b: The Suspension Clause has no extraterritorial reach
opt_c: Habeas is unavailable to non-citizens
opt_d: The DTA provides an adequate substitute in all cases
explanation: Boumediene: Guantanamo detainees enjoy Suspension Clause-protected habeas; DTA's substitute review was inadequate.

stem: Holland v. Florida (2010) held that AEDPA's one-year SOL is subject to:
opt_a: Tolling only for capital cases
opt_b: No tolling whatsoever — the SOL is jurisdictional
opt_c: Tolling only when state collateral-review procedures fail
opt_d: Equitable tolling when extraordinary circumstances and reasonable diligence are shown
explanation: Holland v. Florida: AEDPA's one-year SOL may be equitably tolled. Holland identified attorney misconduct combined with diligence as a basis.

stem: Trump v. Hawaii (2018) addressed standing for individuals and states challenging the third travel-ban executive order. The Court held:
opt_a: Plaintiffs' Establishment Clause claims were generalized grievances
opt_b: Trump v. Hawaii was nonjusticiable as a political question
opt_c: Plaintiffs had standing based on concrete injuries (family separation, university recruitment), permitting merits review
opt_d: States lack standing to challenge presidential immigration decisions
explanation: Trump v. Hawaii: plaintiffs had standing through concrete injuries; the Court reached the merits and upheld the order under rational-basis review.

stem: Trump v. Mazars (2020) addressed congressional subpoenas for the President's financial records. The Court held:
opt_a: Subpoenas for personal records of the sitting President require heightened scrutiny under separation-of-powers analysis
opt_b: Congressional subpoenas are categorically valid
opt_c: Subpoenas require Senate concurrence
opt_d: Mazars-style subpoenas are nonjusticiable political questions
explanation: Trump v. Mazars: balancing test for congressional subpoenas of the sitting President's personal records — heightened separation-of-powers analysis.

stem: Moore v. Harper (2023) addressed the 'independent state legislature theory.' The Court held:
opt_a: The theory was nonjusticiable as a political question
opt_b: State courts cannot review election-law issues at all
opt_c: State legislatures have unreviewable authority over federal elections
opt_d: Federal Elections Clause does not vest exclusive authority in state legislatures; state courts may review legislative election-law actions under state constitutions
explanation: Moore v. Harper: rejected the maximalist independent-state-legislature theory; state courts retain review authority over state-legislature election rules.

stem: In Department of Homeland Security v. New York (2020), the Court addressed the public-charge rule. Standing analysis turned on:
opt_a: Statutory authorization in immigration laws
opt_b: Categorical state standing in immigration cases
opt_c: Whether plaintiff states could show concrete injuries from the regulation's impact on their immigrant populations
opt_d: Parens patriae standing alone
explanation: DHS v. NY: state standing challenges raise concrete-injury questions about specific immigrant-related state programs.

stem: In a False Claims Act qui tam suit, a relator (private citizen) sues on behalf of the U.S. government. Standing exists because:
opt_a: The relator's whistleblower interest provides standing
opt_b: The relator is treated as the government's assignee and shares the government's Article III standing
opt_c: Citizens have inherent standing for fraud against the government
opt_d: FCA citizen-suit provisions categorically confer standing
explanation: FCA qui tam: Vermont Agency of Natural Resources v. United States ex rel. Stevens — relator is treated as the government's assignee for standing purposes.

stem: After TransUnion v. Ramirez (2021), Article III injury requires:
opt_a: Substantial federal-law interest plus injury
opt_b: Either concrete or particularized harm — both not required
opt_c: A statutory grant of citizen-suit standing
opt_d: Concrete harm (with a close historical or common-law analog) — bare statutory violations alone don't suffice
explanation: TransUnion: 'concrete' requires close common-law analog. Bare statutory violations alone don't satisfy Article III.

stem: After Spokeo and TransUnion, intangible harm satisfies Article III injury when:
opt_a: The plaintiff alleges any cognizable interest
opt_b: The harm is shared by many similarly situated
opt_c: It has a close historical or common-law analog (e.g., reputational harm, disclosure of private information)
opt_d: Congress has labeled the harm 'concrete' by statute
explanation: TransUnion: intangible harms must analogize to traditional injuries (defamation, intrusion-on-seclusion, etc.).

stem: A defendant's 'voluntary cessation' of challenged conduct will moot a case only if:
opt_a: It is 'absolutely clear' the conduct will not recur — heavy burden on defendant
opt_b: The court determines cessation in good faith
opt_c: The defendant has settled with the plaintiff
opt_d: The defendant has formally adopted a no-recurrence policy
explanation: Friends of the Earth v. Laidlaw: voluntary cessation rarely moots a case — defendant must show it is 'absolutely clear' the conduct will not recur.

stem: A federal court is asked to facially invalidate a statute as unconstitutional. The plaintiff must show:
opt_a: The statute is invalid in 'all (or nearly all) of its applications,' or — under modern doctrine — has substantial overbreadth
opt_b: The statute violates a fundamental right
opt_c: The statute is unconstitutional as applied to anyone
opt_d: The statute is unconstitutional as applied to plaintiff
explanation: Facial challenges: the Salerno standard ('no set of circumstances' under which the statute is valid) is the high bar; lower for First Amendment overbreadth.

stem: The Trump administration's challenge to nationwide injunctions has generated debate. Whether federal courts have authority to issue universal injunctions:
opt_a: Is governed by 28 U.S.C. § 1652
opt_b: Was settled in 2020 against nationwide injunctions
opt_c: Was settled in 2020 in favor of nationwide injunctions
opt_d: Is contested; the Supreme Court has not definitively addressed the question, though concurring opinions have raised concerns
explanation: Universal injunctions: a contested area. The Supreme Court has not definitively addressed; concurring opinions (Thomas, Gorsuch) have questioned the practice.

stem: Granfinanciera v. Nordberg (1989) held that, where the bankruptcy estate seeks to recover a fraudulent conveyance from a non-creditor:
opt_a: All bankruptcy proceedings require jury trials
opt_b: The defendant has a Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial because the claim is private rather than public
opt_c: Jury rights apply only to involuntary bankruptcy
opt_d: Jury rights depend on the size of the disputed amount
explanation: Granfinanciera: applied 7A jury-trial right where bankruptcy estate sought to recover from a non-creditor — the claim retained its private/common-law character.

stem: Atlas Roofing Co. v. OSHRC (1977) upheld OSHA's civil-penalty adjudication by the OSHRC because:
opt_a: Defendants implicitly consent to non-Article III adjudication
opt_b: Civil penalties under $10,000 don't require jury trials
opt_c: OSHRC adjudication is administrative, not judicial
opt_d: OSHA-created penalties are public rights subject to non-Article III adjudication; the 7A doesn't require jury for public rights
explanation: Atlas Roofing: federally-created public rights may be adjudicated by Article I tribunals; the 7A jury right doesn't apply to public-rights matters.

stem: Mistretta v. United States (1989) upheld the Sentencing Commission's structure, including federal-judge participation. The Court reasoned:
opt_a: Federal judges may serve on any federal commission
opt_b: The Commission is an Article III tribunal
opt_c: Sentencing is exclusively legislative
opt_d: Sentencing-rule promulgation is sufficiently judicial to permit Article III judges' service
explanation: Mistretta: judges' Sentencing Commission service is permissible — sentencing-related work is sufficiently judicial.

stem: Patchak v. Zinke (2018) upheld a federal statute that stripped jurisdiction over a particular pending case (the Match-E-Be-Nash-She-Wish trust-land dispute). The plurality reasoned:
opt_a: Congress can strip jurisdiction over pending cases without violating Klein, so long as it does not direct the rule of decision
opt_b: The statute violated due process
opt_c: The statute exceeded Article I powers
opt_d: All jurisdiction-stripping over pending cases violates Klein
explanation: Patchak: jurisdiction-stripping over pending cases is generally permissible; Klein remains a narrow exception for outcome-rigging.

stem: Atascadero State Hospital v. Scanlon (1985) established that congressional abrogation of state sovereign immunity must be:
opt_a: In a separate statute apart from the substantive provision
opt_b: Approved by state-immunity certification
opt_c: Expressed in unmistakably clear language in the statute
opt_d: Have Senate supermajority approval
explanation: Atascadero: clear-statement rule. Congress must use 'unmistakably clear' language to abrogate state immunity.

stem: Roell v. Withrow (2003) held that consent to magistrate jurisdiction under § 636(c) may be:
opt_a: Withdrawn at any time before final judgment
opt_b: Only at the outset of litigation
opt_c: Only express in writing
opt_d: Inferred from a party's voluntary participation in proceedings before the magistrate after notice of the right to refuse
explanation: Roell v. Withrow: implicit consent — voluntary participation after notice of the right to refuse magistrate jurisdiction can constitute valid consent.

stem: A federal magistrate judge presides over a habeas evidentiary hearing under § 636(b) referral. The district judge must:
opt_a: Conduct de novo review of the magistrate's findings on objected-to portions
opt_b: Conduct clear-error review
opt_c: Refer the matter back for additional findings
opt_d: Adopt the magistrate's findings as the district court's findings
explanation: § 636(b) + Raddatz: de novo review of objected portions of magistrate findings on dispositive matters.

stem: Bowsher v. Synar (1986) struck down a provision making the Comptroller General responsible for executive-budget functions because:
opt_a: Congress lacks authority over the budget
opt_b: The Comptroller General is unconstitutional
opt_c: Congress may not assign executive functions to an officer subject to legislative removal
opt_d: Federal budget caps are non-self-executing
explanation: Bowsher v. Synar: structural separation — Congress cannot reserve executive functions to an officer it controls (the Comptroller General was removable by Congress).

stem: Dames & Moore v. Regan (1981) upheld the President's settlement of Iran-hostage claims via executive agreement. The Court reasoned:
opt_a: Long-standing congressional acquiescence in similar executive practices supported implicit authorization
opt_b: The Hostage Act expressly authorized the settlements
opt_c: Article II grants exclusive presidential authority
opt_d: Executive agreements are fully equivalent to treaties
explanation: Dames & Moore: relied on Youngstown's third tier — congressional acquiescence in long-standing executive practice supported the settlement.

stem: Sprint Communications v. Jacobs (2013) narrowed Younger abstention by holding that, in civil cases, Younger applies only to:
opt_a: 'Exceptional circumstances' — state criminal proceedings, state civil enforcement actions, and state-court orders relating to judicial functions
opt_b: Civil cases involving state agencies
opt_c: All civil cases parallel to ongoing state proceedings
opt_d: Cases where the state expressly invokes Younger
explanation: Sprint: Younger's civil-case application is now limited to three 'exceptional' categories. Most parallel state-federal civil litigation is not subject to Younger abstention.

stem: § 1331 statutory federal-question jurisdiction is read more narrowly than Article III's 'arising under' clause because:
opt_a: The Supreme Court strictly construes congressional jurisdictional grants
opt_b: § 1331 was enacted before modern federal-question doctrine
opt_c: § 1331 incorporates the well-pleaded complaint rule, while Article III reaches any federal ingredient
opt_d: Article III imposes no limit on Congressional jurisdictional choices
explanation: Osborn read Article III broadly (any federal ingredient); Mottley read § 1331 narrowly (well-pleaded complaint).

stem: For diversity-jurisdiction purposes, a corporation incorporated in Delaware with its headquarters and most administrative direction in Texas is a citizen of:
opt_a: Delaware and Texas, under Hertz Corp. v. Friend's nerve-center test
opt_b: Texas only, because principal place of business is the modern test
opt_c: Whichever state has more employees
opt_d: Delaware only, because state of incorporation is the historical test
explanation: Hertz: corporation is citizen of state of incorporation AND state where its 'nerve center' (headquarters/management) is located.

stem: Watson v. Philip Morris narrowed § 1442 federal-officer removal by holding that private contractors are 'acting under' a federal officer only when:
opt_a: They engage in any federally-regulated activity
opt_b: Their relationship resembles principal-agent or close federal supervision — not mere regulatory compliance
opt_c: Their conduct violates federal regulations
opt_d: They are sued by federal officers
explanation: Watson v. Philip Morris: 'acting under' requires close federal supervision, not mere regulatory compliance.

stem: § 1369 confers federal jurisdiction over multiparty mass-disaster cases involving:
opt_a: 75+ deaths from a single accident, with minimal diversity
opt_b: Maritime mass-disaster cases
opt_c: Federally-regulated transportation incidents
opt_d: Class actions involving more than 100 plaintiffs
explanation: § 1369: multiparty multiforum jurisdiction over 75+-death incidents with minimal diversity.

stem: 'Snap removal' refers to a defendant removing under § 1441(b) before being formally served. Lower courts:
opt_a: Have referred the question to Supreme Court certification
opt_b: Have split on whether snap removal is permitted; some allow it on the statute's plain text
opt_c: Have universally rejected snap removal
opt_d: Have universally permitted snap removal
explanation: Snap removal: a contested practice. Circuit courts split on whether the literal 'properly joined and served' language permits the maneuver.

stem: Forum non conveniens (FNC) permits federal courts to dismiss a case in favor of a more convenient foreign forum. The doctrine:
opt_a: Is inapplicable in federal-question cases
opt_b: Differs from § 1404(a) transfer in being applicable to non-U.S. forums and resulting in dismissal
opt_c: Is preempted by § 1404(a)
opt_d: Requires consent of all parties
explanation: FNC: addresses non-U.S. forums (dismissal); § 1404(a) addresses domestic transfers.

stem: Wilton v. Seven Falls Co. (1995) clarified federal-court discretion in declaratory-judgment actions when parallel state proceedings exist. Federal courts have:
opt_a: Discretion only in diversity DJ cases
opt_b: Mandatory jurisdiction in DJ cases
opt_c: Substantial discretion to decline DJ jurisdiction in favor of state proceedings
opt_d: Discretion only after state-court completion
explanation: Wilton: federal courts have substantial discretion in DJ actions when parallel state proceedings exist. Brillhart abstention factors apply.

stem: An insurer files a declaratory-judgment action in federal court seeking a ruling that it has no duty to defend. Under Skelly Oil:
opt_a: FQJ exists if any federal-law issue is presented
opt_b: FQJ exists if the federal-law issue would arise as a defense in the natural action
opt_c: The DJ procedure waives the well-pleaded complaint rule
opt_d: FQJ is determined by the well-pleaded complaint that would have been filed by the natural-coercive-action plaintiff
explanation: Skelly Oil: declaratory-judgment plaintiffs cannot create FQJ via DJ procedure where the underlying coercive suit would not be a federal-question case.

stem: After dismissing all federal claims pretrial, a federal court considers whether to retain jurisdiction over remaining state-law claims. Under § 1367(c):
opt_a: The court may decline supplemental jurisdiction in its discretion, weighing economy, convenience, fairness, and comity
opt_b: The court must retain the state claims if filed alongside the federal claim
opt_c: The court must dismiss the state claims for lack of jurisdiction
opt_d: The court must remand to state court regardless of where the case was filed
explanation: § 1367(c) gives discretion to decline supplemental jurisdiction; Gibbs factors guide the analysis.

stem: A defendant is served on Day 1 with a complaint that is removable. He files notice of removal on Day 33. Plaintiff moves to remand. Under § 1446(b)(1):
opt_a: Removal is timely because the 30-day window runs from service of process, not receipt
opt_b: Remand is proper because removal was filed beyond the 30-day window
opt_c: Remand is improper because the 30-day rule is procedural and waivable
opt_d: Removal is timely because the federal court has discretion to extend
explanation: § 1446(b)(1): notice of removal must be filed within 30 days of receipt of the initial pleading. Procedural and motion-to-remand-waivable, but absent waiver, untimely removal is remanded.

stem: After Sprint Communications v. Jacobs (2013), Younger abstention in civil cases is limited to:
opt_a: Civil cases involving state agencies
opt_b: 'Exceptional circumstances' — state criminal proceedings, state civil enforcement actions, and certain state-court orders relating to judicial functions
opt_c: Cases where the state expressly invokes Younger
opt_d: All civil cases parallel to ongoing state proceedings
explanation: Sprint: Younger's civil-case scope is now limited to three 'exceptional' categories.

stem: Pullman abstention requires federal courts to abstain when:
opt_a: A federal constitutional question depends on an unsettled state-law question that state courts could resolve
opt_b: The federal claim implicates state interests
opt_c: State courts have parallel proceedings
opt_d: The state has expressly invoked abstention
explanation: Pullman: federal courts stay constitutional adjudication if state-court resolution of an unsettled state-law question could obviate the federal issue.

stem: Burford abstention applies when federal-court resolution would:
opt_a: Force state-court litigants to relitigate federal claims
opt_b: Disrupt complex state regulatory schemes involving substantial state interests
opt_c: Implicate state-court interpretations of federal law
opt_d: Conflict with parallel state proceedings
explanation: Burford abstention: federal courts decline jurisdiction to avoid disrupting complex state regulatory frameworks.

stem: Colorado River abstention permits federal courts to defer to parallel state proceedings only:
opt_a: In water-rights cases only
opt_b: In 'exceptional circumstances' weighing comprehensive disposition, convenience, avoidance of piecemeal litigation
opt_c: With consent of all parties
opt_d: Whenever a parallel state proceeding exists
explanation: Colorado River: a narrow abstention doctrine for parallel federal-state litigation. Limited to 'exceptional circumstances.'

stem: The Rooker-Feldman doctrine bars federal-district-court jurisdiction over:
opt_a: Cases brought after state-court judgments became final
opt_b: Cases by state-court losers seeking to overturn state-court judgments
opt_c: Any case touching on a state-court ruling
opt_d: Federal-question cases also litigated in state court
explanation: Rooker-Feldman: federal district courts cannot review state-court judgments. Exxon Mobil narrowed to true 'losers' seeking review.

stem: Atascadero State Hospital v. Scanlon (1985) established that state-immunity waivers must be:
opt_a: Approved by the state attorney general
opt_b: Expressed in 'unmistakably clear' statutory language
opt_c: Adopted by the state legislature
opt_d: In a separate statute apart from the substantive provision
explanation: Atascadero: clear-statement rule. Congress (and states) must use unambiguous language to abrogate or waive state immunity.

stem: Howlett v. Rose held state-law sovereign immunity preempted in § 1983 personal-capacity damages actions because:
opt_a: States have absolute immunity from § 1983 in their own courts
opt_b: Federal-law immunities (Harlow QI) govern § 1983; state-law immunities cannot defeat the federal cause of action
opt_c: § 1983 personal-capacity suits are barred categorically
opt_d: § 1983 incorporates state-law immunities by reference
explanation: Howlett v. Rose: state courts cannot use state-law immunities to defeat § 1983; federal-law immunities govern.

stem: Felder v. Casey held Wisconsin's pre-suit notice requirement preempted in § 1983 actions because:
opt_a: It violated the Equal Protection Clause
opt_b: It conflicted with FRCP 8's pleading standards
opt_c: It exceeded Congress's § 5 abrogation authority
opt_d: It was outcome-determinative and burdened federal-law rights
explanation: Felder: state procedural rules that are outcome-determinative and burden § 1983 rights are preempted in state court.

stem: Indiana ex rel. Anderson v. Brand permitted Supreme Court review of state-law antecedent issues that:
opt_a: Have been overruled by the state's intermediate court
opt_b: Determine federal rights, with deference to but not absolute fidelity to the state-court interpretation
opt_c: Are independent and adequate state grounds
opt_d: Apply only to property cases
explanation: Brand: state-law antecedents that determine federal rights are reviewable with deference. Murdock applies to pure state-law disputes.

stem: Michigan v. Long created a bright-line presumption of federal-law reliance applicable when:
opt_a: The state-court opinion mentions federal law in any capacity
opt_b: The state-court opinion fairly appears to rest on federal law and is not clearly independent
opt_c: The state court certifies a federal question for review
opt_d: The state court explicitly invokes federal grounds
explanation: Long: bright-line presumption favoring federal review unless the state court explicitly invokes independent state grounds.

stem: In a diversity action filed in Pennsylvania federal court, the plaintiff's claim accrued in 2018; PA's 2-year SOL would bar the claim, while a federal default would not. Under Erie/Guaranty Trust:
opt_a: The court applies the more permissive federal default
opt_b: The court applies federal common-law SOL because the case is in federal court
opt_c: The court must apply PA's SOL because state SOLs are substantive for Erie purposes
opt_d: The court applies the SOL of whichever state has the most-significant relationship
explanation: Guaranty Trust v. York: state SOLs are substantive for Erie because applying federal SOLs would alter outcomes.

stem: FRCP 4 authorizes service of process by certain methods. State law in the forum state requires more elaborate service. The federal court applies:
opt_a: Whichever method better protects defendant's due-process rights
opt_b: FRCP 4 only if state law is procedural rather than substantive
opt_c: FRCP 4 because Hanna's two-track makes REA-authorized rules dispositive
opt_d: State service law because service is procedural
explanation: Hanna v. Plumer: REA-authorized FRCP rules apply over conflicting state law without further outcome-determinative analysis.

stem: A defense contractor designs and manufactures a Coast Guard vessel. The vessel's hatch is alleged to be defectively designed; a sailor drowns. Under Boyle's three-part test, the contractor's defense requires:
opt_a: Reasonably precise government specifications, conformance with them, and warning the government of dangers known to the contractor
opt_b: The contract include an express indemnity clause
opt_c: The FTCA expressly authorize the contractor to assert the defense
opt_d: The contractor be a federal agency or instrumentality
explanation: Boyle: three-part test for federal-common-law preemption of state product-liability claims against defense contractors.

stem: In Banco Nacional v. Sabbatino, the Court held the act-of-state doctrine is federal common law applicable to:
opt_a: Recognition of foreign judgments in U.S. courts
opt_b: Treaty-implementation disputes
opt_c: Validity of foreign sovereign acts taken within the foreign sovereign's territory
opt_d: Any commercial transaction involving foreign sovereigns
explanation: Sabbatino: act-of-state generally bars inquiry into the validity of foreign sovereign acts within their own territory.

stem: Walker v. Armco Steel held that, in a diversity action, the federal court should apply:
opt_a: State law governing when a state-law claim is 'commenced' for SOL purposes (service vs filing)
opt_b: FRCP 3 (filing) as the commencement event
opt_c: Federal common law of SOL accrual
opt_d: The longer of state and federal limitations periods
explanation: Walker v. Armco: state law governs SOL commencement (service vs filing) in diversity actions — substantive for Erie.

stem: Stewart Org. v. Ricoh held that 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) controls over conflicting state forum-selection-clause law because:
opt_a: Forum-selection clauses are procedural under Erie
opt_b: § 1404 is part of the FRCP
opt_c: Venue is exclusively federal-law
opt_d: § 1404(a) is a valid federal procedural statute that supplants state law on venue transfer
explanation: Stewart Org. v. Ricoh: federal venue-transfer law controls over conflicting state law.

stem: Gasperini v. Center for Humanities held that a federal court in diversity must apply state law on:
opt_a: Whichever standard is more deferential
opt_b: The substance of jury-verdict review for excessiveness, while applying federal-law review standards
opt_c: Federal-law standards for jury review under FRCP 59
opt_d: Boyle's three-prong contractor defense preempts state tort law
explanation: Gasperini: state law on jury-verdict review affects substantive recovery; the state limit is substantive for Erie.

stem: Shady Grove Orthopedic v. Allstate held that FRCP 23 controls over conflicting state law that limits class-action availability because:
opt_a: Federal courts have discretion to ignore state class-action limits
opt_b: Class actions are uniquely federal
opt_c: FRCP 23 is a valid REA rule that permits class actions in federal court regardless of state limits
opt_d: State limits violate Equal Protection
explanation: Shady Grove: FRCP 23 controls over conflicting state law in diversity, even when state law would bar the class action.

stem: Klaxon v. Stentor held that, in diversity actions, federal courts must apply:
opt_a: The choice-of-law rules of the state in which the federal court sits
opt_b: Federal common-law choice-of-law principles
opt_c: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws
opt_d: The choice-of-law rule of the state with the most significant relationship
explanation: Klaxon: in diversity, federal courts apply forum-state choice-of-law rules. Erie governs choice-of-law as well as substantive law.

stem: In Illinois v. City of Milwaukee (1972), the Court recognized federal common law of:
opt_a: Interstate water pollution disputes
opt_b: Foreign-relations agreements
opt_c: Maritime tort actions
opt_d: Federal banking regulation
explanation: Illinois v. City of Milwaukee: federal common law of interstate water pollution. Subsequently constrained by federal statutes (Clean Water Act).

stem: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents recognized an implied damages remedy for:
opt_a: Fourth Amendment violations by federal officers in the home
opt_b: Eighth Amendment medical claims
opt_c: Fifth Amendment due-process violations
opt_d: First Amendment retaliation
explanation: Bivens's narrow original holding: 4A damages remedy for federal-officer warrantless search.

stem: After Egbert v. Boule, a federal court considering whether to extend Bivens asks:
opt_a: Whether the constitutional right is clearly established
opt_b: Whether special factors counsel hesitation
opt_c: Whether alternative remedies are adequate substitutes for damages
opt_d: Whether any rational reason exists for political-branch handling, in which case extension fails
explanation: Egbert collapsed the test: any rational reason for Congressional preference defeats Bivens extension.

stem: Davis v. Passman extended Bivens because the plaintiff:
opt_a: Was a non-citizen detained at the border
opt_b: Was a NASA engineer demoted for whistleblowing
opt_c: Was a Congressional staffer fired on grounds of sex, and Title VII expressly excluded Congressional staff
opt_d: Was a federal-prison inmate denied medical care
explanation: Davis: Title VII's exclusion of Congressional staff left plaintiff with no federal remedy.

stem: Carlson v. Green held Bivens applied to an Eighth Amendment medical-care claim. The Court reasoned FTCA was insufficient because:
opt_a: FTCA's exclusive-remedy provisions had not yet been enacted
opt_b: Plaintiff sought injunctive relief that FTCA does not authorize
opt_c: Bivens damages directly against officers serve different functions than FTCA recovery (state-law-based)
opt_d: FTCA does not permit suits arising in federal prisons
explanation: Carlson: Bivens and FTCA serve different purposes — Bivens provides uniform federal damages directly against officers.

stem: Cannon v. University of Chicago held Title IX includes an implied PROA for:
opt_a: Both intentional and disparate-impact
opt_b: Intentional sex discrimination, given rights-creating language and Congressional intent
opt_c: Sex discrimination only at federally-funded schools
opt_d: Disparate-impact sex discrimination
explanation: Cannon: Title IX implies PROA for intentional sex discrimination. Sandoval cabined to intentional only.

stem: Sandoval shifted the implied-PROA inquiry from Cort v. Ash's purposivism to:
opt_a: Text and structure of the statute, considering whether Congress used rights-creating language
opt_b: Agency-deference analysis
opt_c: Constitutional-avoidance principles
opt_d: Legislative history primarily
explanation: Sandoval: shifted to textual/structural analysis. Cort's purposivist factors largely displaced.

stem: In Health & Hospital Corp. v. Talevski (2023), the Court held that Spending-Clause statutes can support § 1983 enforcement when:
opt_a: The text uses unambiguous rights-creating language and there is no comprehensive remedial scheme
opt_b: The statute creates categorical entitlement to damages
opt_c: The agency expressly authorizes private suits
opt_d: The state has accepted federal funds
explanation: Talevski: rights-creating language plus absence of an exclusive remedial scheme.

stem: A U.S. Border Patrol agent shoots and kills a Mexican national across the border. The family sues under Bivens. After Hernandez v. Mesa:
opt_a: Bivens applies for the Fourth Amendment but not the Fifth
opt_b: The family's exclusive remedy is diplomatic resolution
opt_c: No Bivens action lies because cross-border shootings present a new context with foreign-relations special factors
opt_d: Bivens applies because the U.S. Constitution reaches federal officers' conduct extraterritorially
explanation: Hernandez v. Mesa: cross-border + foreign-relations special factors defeat Bivens extension.

stem: Bush v. Lucas declined Bivens because:
opt_a: The First Amendment is not self-executing
opt_b: The federal civil-service remedial scheme provided an adequate alternative
opt_c: Government employment is a privilege, not a right
opt_d: Congress had specifically barred Bivens
explanation: Bush v. Lucas: adequate alternative remedies (civil-service review) defeat Bivens extension.

stem: Wilkie v. Robbins declined to extend Bivens to a rancher's harassment claims. A central reason was:
opt_a: Federal officers lack capacity to harass
opt_b: Failure to exhaust administrative remedies
opt_c: Property-rights disputes are exclusively state law
opt_d: The unworkability of an unconstitutional-retaliation Bivens action without a clear standard
explanation: Wilkie v. Robbins: 'special factors' include administrability — a freestanding retaliation claim required courts to police every federal-officer interaction.

stem: Atascadero State Hospital v. Scanlon established that congressional abrogation of state sovereign immunity must:
opt_a: Be in a separate statute
opt_b: Have Senate supermajority approval
opt_c: Be expressed in unmistakably clear language
opt_d: Be approved by state-immunity certification
explanation: Atascadero: clear-statement rule. Congress must use 'unmistakably clear' language to abrogate state immunity.

stem: Edelman v. Jordan distinguishes Ex parte Young from suits seeking:
opt_a: Injunctive relief against state officers in personal capacity
opt_b: Prospective compliance with federal law
opt_c: Declaratory judgment about state federal-law obligations
opt_d: Retroactive money damages payable from the state treasury
explanation: Edelman: Young permits prospective relief but bars retroactive damages from state treasury.

stem: Pennhurst v. Halderman held Ex parte Young does NOT permit:
opt_a: Federal-question suits against state officers
opt_b: Damages claims against state officers in personal capacity
opt_c: Class-action habeas claims
opt_d: Federal-court injunctive enforcement of state law against state officers
explanation: Pennhurst: Young is for federal-law enforcement; using it to enforce state law against state officials would intrude on state sovereignty.

stem: After Seminole Tribe v. Florida, Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity:
opt_a: With state-immunity certification approval
opt_b: Under any enumerated power
opt_c: Whenever it makes a clear statement
opt_d: Only under § 5 of the 14th Amendment, not under Article I commerce powers
explanation: Seminole Tribe: Article I powers cannot abrogate SSI; § 5 of the 14th Amendment can.

stem: Nevada Department of Human Resources v. Hibbs upheld FMLA's family-care provision because:
opt_a: It validly abrogated SSI under § 5 to remedy historical sex discrimination in family leave
opt_b: FMLA is exclusively administrative
opt_c: Commerce-power abrogation is permissible for employment statutes
opt_d: States waived SSI by accepting federal funds
explanation: Hibbs: FMLA family-care provision was upheld as valid § 5 abrogation; congruence-and-proportionality satisfied by historical record.

stem: Tennessee v. Lane held Title II of the ADA validly abrogates SSI as applied to:
opt_a: Cases where the state has accepted federal disability-related funds
opt_b: All disability-discrimination cases against states
opt_c: Cases involving fundamental rights, such as access to courts
opt_d: Only public-employment disability cases
explanation: Lane: Title II abrogation is valid as applied to fundamental-rights cases.

stem: Central Virginia Community College v. Katz upheld bankruptcy proceedings against state interests because:
opt_a: Congress validly abrogated SSI under § 5
opt_b: 11A does not address Article I bankruptcy
opt_c: Bankruptcy is a public-rights matter outside state immunity
opt_d: States consented to federal bankruptcy jurisdiction in the plan of the convention
explanation: Katz: ratification of the Bankruptcy Clause implicitly waived state immunity for bankruptcy proceedings — plan of convention.

stem: Torres v. Texas Department of Public Safety extended plan-of-convention reasoning to:
opt_a: Suits enforcing federal employment protections under congressional war powers
opt_b: Suits enforcing the Equal Protection Clause directly
opt_c: Suits enforcing federal admiralty law against state actors
opt_d: Suits enforcing the Spending Clause
explanation: Torres: USERRA suits against states allowed under war-power-based plan-of-convention abrogation.

stem: Monroe v. Pape held that § 1983 reaches:
opt_a: Only state officials in official capacity
opt_b: State officials acting under color of state law, even when violating state law
opt_c: Federal officials acting jointly with state
opt_d: Only state officials acting within authority
explanation: Monroe: 'under color of' includes abuses of authority. Foundational § 1983 scope.

stem: A county prosecutor authorizes a particular police entry; the entry is later held unconstitutional. Under Pembaur v. Cincinnati:
opt_a: The county is liable only with a pattern of similar decisions
opt_b: The single decision constitutes county 'policy' if the prosecutor was the final policymaker
opt_c: Only the prosecutor personally is liable
opt_d: The county is shielded by sovereign immunity
explanation: Pembaur: a single decision by a final policymaker can constitute Monell 'policy.'

stem: Williams v. Taylor distinguished § 2254(d)(1)'s 'contrary to' from 'unreasonable application' as follows:
opt_a: The two prongs are functionally identical
opt_b: 'Contrary to' applies when the state court reached opposite results on materially indistinguishable facts; 'unreasonable application' applies when the rule was correctly identified but applied unreasonably
opt_c: 'Contrary to' applies to facts; 'unreasonable application' applies to law
opt_d: 'Contrary to' is reviewed de novo; 'unreasonable application' is for clear error
explanation: Williams' canonical articulation. 'Contrary to' = wrong rule or opposite outcome on indistinguishable facts. 'Unreasonable application' = right rule, unreasonable application.

stem: Cullen v. Pinholster restricted federal habeas review under § 2254(d)(1) to:
opt_a: The federal-habeas record, including evidence developed in federal court
opt_b: The record before the state court at the time of its merits adjudication
opt_c: Any record developed in state collateral review
opt_d: The combined state and federal records
explanation: Pinholster: § 2254(d)(1) review limited to the state-court record. New-evidence development sharply curtailed.

stem: A defendant fails to object at trial; the state appellate court denies the claim under a state-procedural-default rule. The federal habeas court should:
opt_a: Apply the cause-and-prejudice standard to determine whether to excuse the procedural default
opt_b: Reach the merits because federal courts review federal claims de novo
opt_c: Apply AEDPA's § 2254(d)(1) standard to the procedural default itself
opt_d: Defer to the state-court procedural ruling without further inquiry
explanation: Wainwright v. Sykes: cause-and-prejudice (or actual-innocence gateway) is the standard for excusing procedural default.

stem: A federal habeas petitioner argues that his collateral-review counsel was ineffective. Under Coleman v. Thompson:
opt_a: Collateral-review attorney error is generally not 'cause' to excuse default, with a narrow Martinez exception
opt_b: Collateral-review attorney error excuses default if the underlying claim is colorable
opt_c: Collateral-review attorney error is always 'cause'
opt_d: Collateral-review counsel's negligence triggers a Strickland-style federal habeas analysis
explanation: Coleman: no constitutional right to collateral-review counsel means error there isn't 'cause' (Martinez carved a narrow exception).

stem: In an Arizona-style state where IATC claims must be raised first on initial-review collateral, the petitioner's collateral-review counsel was ineffective. Under Martinez v. Ryan:
opt_a: Ineffective collateral-review counsel may serve as 'cause' to excuse default of the substantial IATC claim
opt_b: Ineffective collateral-review counsel is never 'cause' under Coleman
opt_c: The procedural default is automatically excused because no other forum was available
opt_d: The petitioner must independently establish actual innocence
explanation: Martinez v. Ryan: where state law channels IATC to initial-review collateral, ineffective counsel there can be 'cause.'

stem: After a state conviction becomes final, the U.S. Supreme Court announces a new constitutional rule of criminal procedure. Under Teague v. Lane:
opt_a: The new rule generally does not apply on collateral review unless it falls within the substantive-rule or watershed-procedural exceptions
opt_b: The new rule applies on collateral review only to capital cases
opt_c: The new rule applies on collateral review only if the state expressly opts in
opt_d: The new rule applies on collateral review because federal courts apply current constitutional law
explanation: Teague: new procedural rules don't apply retroactively on collateral review except for narrow exceptions.

stem: A state prisoner whose Fourth Amendment exclusionary-rule claim was fully litigated in state court seeks federal habeas relief. Under Stone v. Powell:
opt_a: Federal habeas review proceeds de novo because 4A claims are core
opt_b: Federal habeas relief is available only if the petitioner is actually innocent
opt_c: Federal habeas relief is unavailable because the state provided full and fair litigation opportunity
opt_d: Federal habeas review proceeds under § 2254(d)(1) deference
explanation: Stone v. Powell: 4A exclusionary-rule claims aren't cognizable on habeas where the state gave full and fair litigation opportunity.

stem: A petitioner files federal habeas more than one year after conviction-finality but produces credible new affidavits showing actual innocence. Under McQuiggin v. Perkins:
opt_a: The actual-innocence showing equitably tolls AEDPA's one-year statute of limitations
opt_b: The petition must be dismissed because AEDPA's SOL is jurisdictional
opt_c: The petitioner must produce DNA evidence
opt_d: Actual innocence excuses procedural default but not the AEDPA SOL
explanation: McQuiggin: actual-innocence claim under Schlup serves as an equitable gateway through AEDPA's SOL.

stem: In Boumediene v. Bush, the Court held:
opt_a: The Suspension Clause limits Congress's ability to strip habeas jurisdiction over Guantanamo detainees
opt_b: The Suspension Clause has no extraterritorial reach
opt_c: The DTA provides an adequate substitute in all cases
opt_d: Habeas is unavailable to non-citizens
explanation: Boumediene: Guantanamo detainees enjoy Suspension Clause-protected habeas; DTA's substitute review was inadequate.

stem: Brecht v. Abrahamson (1993) established the harmless-error standard for federal habeas review of constitutional errors. The standard:
opt_a: Categorical reversal absent harmless beyond reasonable doubt
opt_b: Wainwright's cause-and-prejudice standard governs procedural default
opt_c: Substantial and injurious effect on the verdict (a more deferential standard than Chapman's harmless-beyond-a-reasonable-doubt)
opt_d: Plain-error review under FRCP 52
explanation: Brecht v. Abrahamson: federal habeas applies the more deferential 'substantial and injurious effect' standard, not Chapman's stricter direct-review standard.

stem: Pre-enforcement First Amendment standing requires the plaintiff to show:
opt_a: Substantial likelihood of injury to a third party
opt_b: Intent to engage in arguably proscribed conduct, with a credible threat of enforcement
opt_c: Past prosecution under the challenged statute
opt_d: Express agency intent to prosecute
explanation: SBA List v. Driehaus: pre-enforcement standing for chilled speech requires intent + credible threat.

stem: A class-action plaintiff alleges economic harm from a federal regulation. Within the same class are members harmed and unharmed. The Court's TransUnion decision suggests:
opt_a: Class certification automatically confers standing on all members
opt_b: Class certification is barred if any member is unharmed
opt_c: Each class member must independently satisfy Article III injury — class certification cannot save unharmed members
opt_d: The Article III injury requirement is satisfied if at least one class member is harmed
explanation: TransUnion: every class member must individually have Article III standing for damages awards. The Court has narrowed class-wide standing significantly.

stem: In a facial First Amendment overbreadth challenge, the plaintiff has standing to assert:
opt_a: Only her own First Amendment rights
opt_b: Third-party rights only with their consent
opt_c: Both her rights and third-party rights with statutory authorization
opt_d: Third-party speakers' rights, because the law's chilling effect on others is itself a constitutional injury
explanation: First Amendment overbreadth: plaintiffs may challenge laws facially even where their own conduct is unprotected, because of the chilling effect on third parties.

stem: INS v. Chadha (1983) struck down the legislative-veto provision because it failed:
opt_a: Express enumerated-power authority
opt_b: Due process for affected aliens
opt_c: Bicameralism and presentment under Article I § 7
opt_d: The political-question doctrine
explanation: Chadha: legislative-veto provisions violate Article I's bicameralism and presentment requirements.

stem: Plaut v. Spendthrift Farm (1995) struck down a federal statute that:
opt_a: Created a new federal cause of action retroactively
opt_b: Stripped jurisdiction over pending cases
opt_c: Modified the statute of limitations retroactively
opt_d: Reopened final federal-court judgments to permit additional litigation
explanation: Plaut v. Spendthrift Farm: Congress cannot reopen final federal judgments — that violates Article III's structural separation.

stem: Sprint Communications v. Jacobs (2013) narrowed Younger abstention by holding that, in civil cases, Younger applies only to:
opt_a: 'Exceptional circumstances' — state criminal proceedings, state civil enforcement actions, and state-court orders relating to judicial functions
opt_b: Cases where the state expressly invokes Younger
opt_c: All civil cases parallel to ongoing state proceedings
opt_d: Civil cases involving state agencies
explanation: Sprint: Younger's civil-case scope is now limited to three 'exceptional' categories.

stem: The Anti-Injunction Act (28 U.S.C. § 2283) generally bars federal courts from enjoining state-court proceedings except:
opt_a: When the state proceeding violates Equal Protection
opt_b: When expressly authorized by Congress, in aid of jurisdiction, or to protect/effectuate federal judgments
opt_c: When the state-court judgment would be res judicata in federal court
opt_d: Whenever federal law preempts state law
explanation: § 2283: three express exceptions. Mitchum v. Foster held § 1983 is 'expressly authorized' for this purpose.

stem: Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Industries (2005) narrowed Rooker-Feldman by holding it applies only to:
opt_a: Federal claims that should have been raised in state court
opt_b: Cases parallel to ongoing state proceedings
opt_c: Any case touching on a state-court determination
opt_d: Cases brought by state-court losers complaining of injuries caused by state-court judgments and inviting district-court review
explanation: Exxon Mobil v. Saudi Basic: Rooker-Feldman is narrow — applies only to true 'losers' seeking direct district-court review.

stem: Wilton v. Seven Falls Co. (1995) clarified federal-court discretion in declaratory-judgment actions when:
opt_a: The federal court is the only available forum
opt_b: No parallel state action exists
opt_c: Both parties consent to federal jurisdiction
opt_d: A parallel state-court action is pending — federal courts have substantial discretion to decline DJ jurisdiction
explanation: Wilton: federal courts have substantial discretion in DJ actions when parallel state proceedings exist. Brillhart abstention factors apply.

stem: Mitchum v. Foster (1972) held that § 1983 is an 'expressly authorized' exception to the Anti-Injunction Act, permitting:
opt_a: Federal-court intervention in state criminal proceedings
opt_b: Federal-court injunctive relief against state-court proceedings in § 1983 actions
opt_c: Removal of all § 1983 cases
opt_d: Federal-court appellate review of state judgments
explanation: Mitchum v. Foster: § 1983 fits within the 'expressly authorized' exception of § 2283. (Younger abstention still constrains in many situations.)

stem: Tafflin v. Levitt (1990) addressed concurrent state-court jurisdiction over RICO claims. The Court held:
opt_a: State courts have concurrent jurisdiction over RICO claims absent express Congressional preemption
opt_b: Concurrent jurisdiction depends on whether the state has analogous racketeering law
opt_c: Concurrent jurisdiction depends on the state's waiver of sovereign immunity
opt_d: RICO claims are exclusively federal
explanation: Tafflin v. Levitt: presumption of concurrent state-court jurisdiction over federal claims absent express preemption.

stem: Yellow Freight v. Donnelly held that state courts may not refuse to entertain Title VII employment-discrimination claims, even though Congress provided exclusive federal-court jurisdiction in some other contexts. The presumption:
opt_a: Concurrent state-court jurisdiction is unavailable absent express Congressional grant
opt_b: Concurrent state-court jurisdiction depends on the state's waiver
opt_c: Concurrent state-court jurisdiction is exclusive to civil rights cases
opt_d: Concurrent state-court jurisdiction exists over federal claims unless Congress expressly preempts
explanation: Yellow Freight (and Tafflin): strong presumption of concurrent state-court jurisdiction.

stem: Atlas Roofing Co. v. OSHRC (1977) upheld OSHA's civil-penalty adjudication by the OSHRC because:
opt_a: Civil penalties under $10,000 don't require jury trials
opt_b: OSHRC adjudication is administrative, not judicial
opt_c: Defendants implicitly consent to non-Article III adjudication
opt_d: OSHA-created penalties are public rights subject to non-Article III adjudication; the 7A doesn't require jury for public rights
