explanation: Atlas Roofing: federally-created public rights may be adjudicated by Article I tribunals; the 7A doesn't apply to public-rights matters.

stem: Granfinanciera v. Nordberg (1989) held that, where the bankruptcy estate seeks to recover a fraudulent conveyance from a non-creditor:
opt_a: Jury rights depend on the size of the disputed amount
opt_b: Jury rights apply only to involuntary bankruptcy
opt_c: The defendant has a Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial because the claim is private rather than public
opt_d: All bankruptcy proceedings require jury trials
explanation: Granfinanciera: applied 7A jury-trial right where bankruptcy estate sought to recover from a non-creditor.

stem: In O'Melveny & Myers v. FDIC (1994), the Court refused to fashion a federal common-law rule of attorney-client privilege protective of FDIC's interests. The reasoning:
opt_a: FDIC has waived such interests by statute
opt_b: Federal common law cannot affect tort claims
opt_c: Federal common law is disfavored where Congress has provided no clear authorization and uniformity is not essential
opt_d: Attorney-client privilege is uniquely state-law
explanation: O'Melveny & Myers: federal common law disfavored absent clear federal interest and Congressional warrant.

stem: In Wilkie v. Robbins (2007), the Court declined to extend Bivens to a rancher's harassment claims against BLM officers. A central reason was:
opt_a: The unworkability of an unconstitutional-retaliation Bivens action without a clear standard
opt_b: Federal officers lack capacity to harass
opt_c: Property-rights disputes are exclusively state law
opt_d: Failure to exhaust administrative remedies
explanation: Wilkie v. Robbins: 'special factors' include administrability — a freestanding retaliation claim required courts to police every federal-officer interaction.

stem: In a hypothetical post-Egbert case, a federal officer searches a U.S. citizen's home without a warrant; the citizen sues under Bivens. The court is most likely to:
opt_a: Refer the case to administrative review
opt_b: Apply qualified immunity without Bivens analysis
opt_c: Find Bivens applies because the case fits squarely within the original 4A-in-the-home context
opt_d: Decline Bivens because Egbert collapsed the analysis
explanation: Bivens's core 4A-in-the-home context survives Egbert. The original holding remains good law; only extensions are barred.

stem: After Egbert, even Fourth Amendment Bivens claims face headwinds outside the original Bivens factual context. This means:
opt_a: Bivens applies to all 4A claims
opt_b: Bivens applies only with consent of the U.S. as defendant
opt_c: Bivens is largely confined to the original Bivens, Davis, and Carlson cases — extensions almost always fail
opt_d: Bivens has been formally overruled
explanation: Egbert effectively bars almost all Bivens extensions; only the three original contexts remain reliable.

stem: Alden v. Maine (1999) extended state sovereign immunity to:
opt_a: Suits against states in their own courts under congressional commerce power
opt_b: Suits brought by foreign sovereigns
opt_c: Suits in federal court only
opt_d: Suits seeking injunctive relief
explanation: Alden v. Maine: state sovereign immunity extends to state-court jurisdiction. SSI is a structural principle, not just an 11A textual matter.

stem: Connick v. Thompson (2011) narrowed Canton failure-to-train liability by holding that:
opt_a: A single Brady violation is insufficient to establish a pattern of deliberate indifference
opt_b: Failure-to-train is unavailable for unconstitutional acts in good faith
opt_c: Failure-to-train requires sustained negligent supervision over years
opt_d: Failure-to-train is unavailable for prosecutorial decisions
explanation: Connick: Canton failure-to-train requires a pattern; even egregious single violations are insufficient.

stem: Owen v. City of Independence (1980) held that municipalities, unlike individual officers, do NOT enjoy:
opt_a: Absolute immunity from § 1983 injunctive relief
opt_b: Statutory immunity from § 1983 attorney-fee awards
opt_c: Sovereign immunity from § 1983 damages claims
opt_d: Good-faith / qualified immunity from § 1983 damages claims
explanation: Owen v. City of Independence: municipalities lack QI under § 1983 — different policy considerations.

stem: Brecht v. Abrahamson (1993) established the harmless-error standard for federal habeas review of constitutional errors. The standard:
opt_a: Substantial and injurious effect on the verdict (more deferential than Chapman's beyond-a-reasonable-doubt)
opt_b: Plain-error review under FRCP 52
opt_c: Categorical reversal absent harmless beyond reasonable doubt
opt_d: The state court adjudicated on the merits, triggering AEDPA deference
explanation: Brecht: federal habeas applies the more deferential 'substantial and injurious effect' standard.

stem: Holland v. Florida (2010) held that AEDPA's one-year SOL is subject to:
opt_a: No tolling whatsoever
opt_b: Equitable tolling when extraordinary circumstances and reasonable diligence are shown
opt_c: Tolling only when state collateral procedures fail
opt_d: Tolling only for capital cases
explanation: Holland v. Florida: AEDPA's SOL is subject to equitable tolling. Holland identified attorney misconduct + diligence.

stem: Heck v. Humphrey (1994) held that a § 1983 plaintiff seeking damages for an alleged unconstitutional conviction:
opt_a: May proceed in either § 1983 or habeas, at her election
opt_b: Must exhaust state-court remedies before any federal suit
opt_c: Must elect to forgo damages and seek injunctive relief
opt_d: Must first secure invalidation of the conviction (e.g., through habeas) before bringing the § 1983 suit
explanation: Heck v. Humphrey: § 1983 damages claim that would necessarily imply conviction's invalidity is not cognizable until the conviction has been invalidated.
